one_line_takeaway: VMI shows that a single imperceptibly perturbed image, once it enters an LVLM's persistent multi-turn context, can be optimized (via benign anchoring plus context-cycling) to stay silent for dozens of unrelated turns and then reliably steer the model toward an attacker-chosen recommendation (financial, political, or commercial) the moment a matching trigger topic comes up, and this behavior transfers to fine-tuned downstream models without further attacker optimization.
key_finding: VMI achieves substantial combined success rates (SR^) across all three tested models and all four attack targets, including targets referencing a non-existent product (the 'Apple iCar'), where models often hallucinate supporting justification for the fabricated recommendation. The attack remains effective even after 27 conversation turns / >10,000 tokens of unrelated intervening dialogue, and generalizes to fully held-out context prompt sets (Diverse, Holiday) not seen during optimization, and to paraphrased anchor/trigger prompts, with only a slight drop in success rate. Ablations show the single-turn baseline (direct adaptation of prior single-turn attacks) fails almost completely beyond the first conversational turn and has very low SR_context (severe leakage of the target into unrelated turns); adding benign anchoring substantially improves SR_context for short conversations but does not generalize across context lengths; adding a fixed-length context further improves robustness across lengths; and the full VMI (anchoring + context-cycling) achieves the best results overall, particularly after several conversation turns. Across models, Qwen3-VL is generally the most robust to VMI, Qwen2.5-VL is more susceptible, and LLaVA-OneVision-1.5 is the most susceptible on most context sets (though it is the least susceptible model specifically on the coherent 'Holiday' context). Perturbations optimized only on the Qwen3-VL base model transfer with similarly high success rates to its fine-tuned derivatives SEA-LION and QoQ-Med3 without any further optimization on those models, demonstrating a gray-box threat against proprietary/downstream fine-tunes built on a public base checkpoint.
core_contribution: The paper introduces Visual Memory Injection (VMI), a targeted adversarial-image attack against large vision-language models (LVLMs) in multi-turn conversation settings. It exploits the fact that an uploaded image persists in an LVLM's context across the whole conversation. VMI adds an imperceptible L_inf perturbation to an image such that the model behaves normally on all unrelated prompts but, once a user later asks a topic-specific 'trigger' question (e.g. a stock or product recommendation), the model emits an adversary-chosen target message. Two mechanisms make this work: (i) benign behavioral anchoring, which jointly optimizes the perturbation to produce a correct/helpful first-turn response (e.g. a normal image caption or landmark identification) alongside the malicious n-th-turn target response, so the attack does not degenerate into always emitting the target; and (ii) context-cycling, which during optimization periodically varies the length of the conversational context fed to the model (cycling from a minimal 2-turn context up to an n-turn context every tau=5 iterations), so the learned perturbation generalizes across arbitrary conversation lengths at test time rather than overfitting to one fixed context length. The perturbation is optimized via APGD (Croce & Hein) under an L_inf budget of epsilon=8/255, using at most n=8 turns during optimization but evaluated at up to 27 turns at inference.
threat_model: Attacker uploads a single manipulated (but visually imperceptibly perturbed) image to a public platform (social media, stock photo sites). A benign user independently downloads/finds this image and feeds it to an LVLM chatbot as the first turn of a multi-turn conversation, then continues chatting on unrelated topics for many turns. The attacker has white-box access to the target model to construct the perturbation (gradient-based APGD optimization) but no control over what the user asks in intervening turns; transferability to gray-box fine-tuned deployments (attacker has only the public base model, not the deployed fine-tune) is also evaluated. The attack is designed to be stealthy: on non-trigger prompts the model must behave normally so the user has no reason to suspect manipulation, and the malicious output only fires when the user happens to ask about the adversary's chosen trigger topic (e.g. stock advice, a political-voting question, a product recommendation).
limitations: The authors explicitly state two limitations: (1) the attack requires white-box access to a base model for perturbation construction; attacking models available only through an API remains an open, unaddressed challenge (only gray-box transfer to fine-tuned variants of a known base model was tested, not fully black-box API attacks). (2) The method as evaluated restricts conversations to a single input image; multi-image conversations are not studied. Additionally (noted by the reviewer from the paper's own described setup, not stated as a limitation by the authors): the automated success metric relies on brittle keyword string-matching (positive/negative keyword lists per target) validated on only a small, non-random user study sample (28+28 turns, 4 participants) drawn exclusively from conversations the metric already scored as successful, which could overstate metric reliability since it does not sample from metric-negative cases or measure false-negative rates; and generation is done at temperature 0.6 with top-p 0.95, so reported success rates reflect one particular stochastic decoding configuration rather than a swept range.

datasets_used: COCO (Lin et al., 2014) - 20 randomly sampled images used as benign anchor images for the phone/car targets
LMARKS - self-collected set of 20 'striking but not very well known landmark' images, used for the political/stock targets
Diverse* context prompt set - 25 prompts, generated with Gemini-fast, first 6 partially used during attack optimization
Diverse context prompt set - 25 prompts, generated with ChatGPT-5.2, fully held out from optimization
Holiday context prompt set - 25 prompts forming a single coherent trip-planning conversation, generated with Gemini-2.5-flash, fully held out from optimization
quantitative_results: Perturbation budget: L_infinity radius epsilon = 8/255, 2000 APGD optimization iterations

Context-cycling: max context length 8 turns during optimization, cycling period tau = 5 iterations; test-time evaluation extends to up to 27 conversation turns / >10,000 tokens (vs. max 8 turns used at optimization time)

Evaluation metric validation user study: 100% agreement rate (4 participants) that target-turn responses exhibited the described target behavior; 95.2% of context turns with no flagged keyword were separately rated as helpful/appropriate responses

Ablation on Qwen3-VL stock target: 'single target' baseline (adaptation of Schlarmann & Hein 2023) fails almost completely beyond the first turn and shows the worst (lowest) SR_context due to target leakage into unrelated context turns; adding benign anchoring ('w/o cycle & context') substantially improves SR_context for short conversations but does not generalize across context lengths; adding a fixed 8-turn context ('w/o cycle') improves SR^ considerably across context lengths; full VMI (anchoring + context-cycling) achieves the highest SR_target and matches 'w/o cycle' on SR_context, yielding the best overall SR^ especially at longer conversation lengths

Transfer attack: perturbations optimized on Qwen3-VL and evaluated without further optimization on fine-tuned derivatives Qwen-SEA-LION-v4-8B-VL and QoQ-Med3-VL-8B achieve success rates similar to the source model on the stock recommendation target
Model parameter sizes (Table 3): Qwen2.5-VL-7B-Instruct = 8.3B total, Qwen3-VL-8B-Instruct = 8.8B total, LLaVA-OneVision-1.5-8B-Instruct = 8.5B total
Generation hyperparameters: temperature 0.6, top-p 0.95, image resolution 224x224, max 512 new tokens per turn
baselines_compared: 'single target' - direct adaptation of the single-turn targeted attack from Schlarmann & Hein (2023), no benign anchoring, no multi-turn context used during optimization
'w/o cycle & context' - adds benign anchoring (first-turn anchor prompt/target) to the single-target objective but no extended context
'w/o cycle' - adds benign anchoring plus a fixed 8-turn conversational context during optimization (no cycling of context length)
Full VMI - benign anchoring + context-cycling (the paper's proposed method)
